column1: In low-resolution WSI, esophageal cancer with low risk, characterized by well-contained tumor areas with smooth boundaries, uniform pink-purple staining, and preserved overall tissue architecture.
In low-resolution WSI, esophageal cancer with intermediate-low risk, tumors show mild architectural disruption and moderate stromal presence, with some texture variation but limited necrosis.

In low-resolution WSI, esophageal cancer with intermediate-high risk, regions display more irregular, infiltrative growth, increased cellular density, patchy necrosis, and heterogeneous color distribution.
In low-resolution WSI, esophageal cancer with high risk, tumor regions are chaotic and invasive with indistinct margins, extensive necrosis, dense blue-purple staining, and coarse texture throughout.

In high-resolution WSI, esophageal cancer with low risk, tumor cells are well-differentiated with uniform, small nuclei, low mitotic activity, minimal pleomorphism, and preserved glandular or squamous architecture.

In high-resolution WSI, esophageal cancer with intermediate-low risk, cells show mild-to-moderate nuclear atypia, scattered mitoses, early glandular or epithelial disorganization, and focal stromal response.

In high-resolution WSI, esophageal cancer with intermediate-high risk, tumor nests exhibit moderate-to-severe nuclear pleomorphism, increased mitotic figures (including atypical mitoses), and disrupted architecture with reactive stroma.

In high-resolution WSI, esophageal cancer with high risk, poorly differentiated or undifferentiated tumor cells display severe pleomorphism, hyperchromatic nuclei, abundant abnormal mitoses, necrotic areas, and intense desmoplastic or inflammatory stromal reaction.
